title: AI is driving divergent growth trends across US metros
gist: AI is widening economic divergence across US metros: tech- and professional-services-heavy cities (NY, SF, Seattle) plus academic hubs (Madison, Boulder) lead GDP growth, but job gains lag and the top six metros now hold over half of national tech-services GDP.

insights.claim: The top six US metros now account for over half of national tech-services GDP, reflecting growing concentration of economic power in a handful of cities.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Concentration favors CRE, infrastructure, and equity exposure tied to leading tech metros over broad-based regional plays.
insights.quote: the top six metros now account for over half of the national tech-services GDP
insights.locator: While AI is expected to enhance productivity

insights.claim: Major metros such as New York, San Francisco, and Seattle are leading in GDP growth, driven by high concentrations of tech and professional services.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Tilt regional real estate and labor-market exposure toward established tech-services hubs benefiting from AI.
insights.locator: Major metros like New York, San Francisco

insights.claim: Midsize cities such as Madison and Boulder are emerging as strong economic players by leveraging academic institutions to foster innovation and tech-driven enterprises.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Academic-anchored midsize metros offer secondary AI-growth plays for regional investment and talent sourcing.
insights.locator: Midsize cities such as Madison and Boulder

insights.claim: AI is boosting GDP growth in leading metros, but job gains have not kept pace, signaling productivity-led rather than employment-led expansion.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Watch for jobless-style growth in tech metros; margins and productivity may rise faster than local labor demand.
insights.locator: Major metros like New York, San Francisco

insights.claim: Immigration trends and demographic shifts are influencing US metro job markets more significantly than AI in the short term.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Near-term labor-market forecasts should weight migration and demographics over AI displacement narratives.
insights.quote: Factors such as immigration trends and demographic shifts are influencing job markets more significantly than AI in the short term.
insights.locator: While AI is expected to enhance productivity
